coredata.dc:title: Potential usage of boron modified carbon nanodots as a marker candidate for coronavirus disease (COVID-19) antibody detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wibrianto, Aswandi
Saputra, Yudha J.
Sugito, Siti F.A.
Khairunisa, Siti Q.
Rachman, Brian E.
Nasronudin, Nasronudin
Megasari, Ni Luh A.
Chang, Jia-yaw
Fahmi, Mochamad Z.
coredata.dc:description: The global outbreak of COVID-19 in December 2019 has highlighted rapid and accurate diagnostic tools for effective intervention. While the RT-PCR test offers 86 % sensitivity, uncertainties often require supplementary screening. This research investigates how carbon dots (CDs) can be utilized as markers for COVID-19 antibodies, taking advantage of their biocompatibility and low toxicity. CDs were synthesized using citric acid (CA) and APBA with boronic acid, enabling the detection of COVID-19 IgG antibodies with increased absorbance and fluorescence. Comprehensive analyses confirmed the successful synthesis of APBA-CDs, prompting further exploration of their impact on SARS-CoV-2 RNA. Increased absorbance levels were observed in categories K1, K2, and K3, attributed to the introduction of CDs into plasma, indicating effective binding of APBA-CDs to COVID-19 antibodies. In addition, the fluorescence tests consistently showed heightened levels across all categories, emphasizing the effective binding of APBA-CDs with COVID-19 antibodies, particularly in positive plasma samples. As a part of our analysis, we conducted a PCA test to validate the data, which revealed that APBA-CDs are specific to IgG+ antibodies. The results showed a sensitivity rate of 74 % and a specificity rate of 53 %, while, when tested for IgM antibodies, the sensitivity and specificity rates were 63 % and 27 %, respectively. These findings highlight the potential of APBA-CDs as a sensitive and specific marker for COVID-19 antibody detection, offering potential for diagnostic tool development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boron doping
COVID-19
IgG antibody
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Marker Assay on COVID-19 Virus Antibodies
Assessment of APBCA-CDs Efficacy in Targeting COVID-19 Virus Antibodies
Statistical Evaluation of APBA-CDs using ANOVA and PCA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption (black), excitation (red), and emission (blue) UV–vis/PL spectra of APBA-CDs, equipped with fluorescence image of APBA-CDs under 365 nm UV light. (b) PL emission spectra of APBA-CDs under varied excitation wavelength sources (360 – 500 nm).

Morphological properties of as-product APBA-CDs. (a) TEM image supported with (b) particle size distribution of APBA-CDs. (c) 2D AFM and (d) SAED photographs of APBA-CDs (e) XRD pattern and (f) Raman spectra of APBA-CDs. (g) FTIR transmittances of APBA (blue) reactants, citric acid (black), and as-prepared APBA-CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) B1s, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s in APBA-CDs.

Absorption comparison bar graphs between APBA-CDs (under varied excitation wavelengths from (a) 360, (b) 400, (c) 420, and (d) 450 nm, and plasma blood (negative control) on COVID-19 Virus Antibodies.
Emission comparison bar graphs between APBA-CDs (using excitation wavelength of 360 nm) and plasma blood (negative control) on COVID-19 Virus Antibodies.
Statistical analysis of APBA-CDs as an COVID-19 antibody detection using (a) ANOVA and (b) PCA approaches.

head.ce:title: Potential usage of boron modified carbon nanodots as a marker candidate for coronavirus disease (COVID-19) antibody detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indonesia Supra Modification Nano-Micro Engineering Research Group, Universitas Airlangga

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The global outbreak of COVID-19 in December 2019 has highlighted rapid and accurate diagnostic tools for effective intervention. While the RT-PCR test offers 86 % sensitivity, uncertainties often require supplementary screening. This research investigates how carbon dots (CDs) can be utilized as markers for COVID-19 antibodies, taking advantage of their biocompatibility and low toxicity. CDs were synthesized using citric acid (CA) and APBA with boronic acid, enabling the detection of COVID-19 IgG antibodies with increased absorbance and fluorescence. Comprehensive analyses confirmed the successful synthesis of APBA-CDs, prompting further exploration of their impact on SARS-CoV-2 RNA. Increased absorbance levels were observed in categories K1, K2, and K3, attributed to the introduction of CDs into plasma, indicating effective binding of APBA-CDs to COVID-19 antibodies. In addition, the fluorescence tests consistently showed heightened levels across all categories, emphasizing the effective binding of APBA-CDs with COVID-19 antibodies, particularly in positive plasma samples. As a part of our analysis, we conducted a PCA test to validate the data, which revealed that APBA-CDs are specific to IgG+ antibodies. The results showed a sensitivity rate of 74 % and a specificity rate of 53 %, while, when tested for IgM antibodies, the sensitivity and specificity rates were 63 % and 27 %, respectively. These findings highlight the potential of APBA-CDs as a sensitive and specific marker for COVID-19 antibody detection, offering potential for diagnostic tool development.

ce:section.ce:para: The first case of coronavirus disease (COVID-19) in humans was discovered in Wuhan City, China. This disease is caused by SARS-CoV-2. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), in 2020, COVID-19 is the third disease caused by a coronavirus, spreading faster and more extensively than SARS and MERS. Studies show that COVID-19 patients admitted to the hospital experience severity as low as 1.4 % and as high as 61.5 % in critical stages [1,2]. According to data compiled by the Center for Systems Science and Engineering (CSSE) at Johns Hopkins University (JHU), as of January 12th, 2022, there have been over 314.019.135 confirmed cases of COVID-19 since the first cases were discovered in 2019, resulting in 5507,370 deaths [3]. In general, COVID-19 symptoms include fever, headache, fatigue, diarrhea, loss of taste and smell, and a dry cough that can lead to respiratory distress and death [4,5]. A recent study by Geneva University Hospitals found that symptoms of SARS-CoV-2 infection typically appear 2–3 days after infection, and about 32 % of participants reported experiencing at least one symptom. Symptoms can affect functional capacity up to 12 months post-infection [5]. As of April 2020, there were 1790 confirmed cases of COVID-19, with 170 deaths and 112 recoveries [6]. Indonesia experienced a sharp surge in COVID-19 cases from December 2020 to February 2021, with a peak of 14,224 confirmed cases in a single day on January 16, 2021. In the second wave of COVID-19 spread on July 15, 2021, the number of cases increased dramatically to 56,757 per day [7].

A highly effective method for detecting a COVID-19 infection is the Real Time-Polymerase Chain Reaction (RT-PCR) test, widely considered the gold standard. The sensitivity rate of the test is 86 %, however, a more comprehensive diagnosis may be necessary [8]. The sensitivity of RT-PCR increases significantly after 3–7 days of COVID-19 infection, which can lead to early diagnosis, reducing the chances of severe symptoms and death [9]. The high cost of COVID-19 RT-PCR tests in Indonesia is due to the reliance on imported tools, materials, and reagents. In addition, a CT-Scan test is necessary to confirm the presence of COVID-19 after the RT-PCR test has been conducted [10]. However, the CT scan results for COVID-19 can resemble those of pneumonia and other respiratory tract diseases, and the radiation exposure from performing a CT scan can be harmful, especially to children and pregnant women [11]. This research aims to create an affordable, precise, and efficient virus detection method. The proposed detection method involves a biosensor that uses carbon dots (CDs), which have strong absorbance in the UV region (230–320 nm) [12]. The characteristics of CDs make them suitable as biosensors or markers for COVID-19. CDs are highly absorbent CDs, or cyclodextrins, and are a type of carbon nanoparticle less than 10 nm in size. They have excellent absorbent properties due to their strong absorbance, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and solubility. Additionally, CDs are environmentally friendly and easily soluble in various solutions. The unique properties of CDs make them promising in various health fields, such as biosensors, drug delivery, and bioimaging. In addition, CDs are easy to synthesize at a low cost and are resistant to photobleaching and photodegradation [13].

The pyrolysis method can synthesize CDs that perform as a promising COVID-19 sensor. For instance, magnetic carbon nanocomposites have emerged as promising candidates for biosensor applications [14]. Furthermore, CDs derived from combining citric acid (CA) and boronic acid showed potential applications in treating, preventing HIV, and suppressing cancer through in vivo studies [15]. Additionally, CDs synthesized from boronic acid demonstrate efficacy as sensors for detecting the COVID-19 virus [16–18]. Expanding the spectrum of applications, Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) exhibit versatility in detecting various virus strains and present a prospective role as a broad-spectrum antiviral therapy, particularly in combatting COVID-19.

The present study focuses on synthesizing CDs using CA and 2-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA). CA is a carbon source with a carboxyl group, facilitating essential dehydration and carbonization reactions. CA exhibits respectable biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity in healthy cells [19]. APBA is a carbon and nitrogen source during the CDs synthesis. This strategic combination of CA and APBA contributes to successfully fabricating CDs with desirable properties for potential biomedical applications [20]. Nanoparticles (i.e., quantum dots, gold nanoparticles (GNPs), and silver nanoparticles) have been widely used in COVID-19 antibody marker assays [21,22]. The alteration of these nanoparticles' optical properties is a reliable indicator for detection. For instance, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) have demonstrated changes in absorption and emission when in contact with IgG or IgM antibodies [4]. This phenomenon has been extensively studied, showcasing the potential for precise detection methods. However, exploring modified CDs absorption and emission changes in the presence of IgG and IgM antibodies for COVID-19 detection remains uncharted territory and emerges a notable gap in research that warrants investigation. The present study will examine alterations in the absorption and emission of Boron-modified CDs in the presence of IgG and IgM antibodies, which could significantly contribute to filling this gap. By demonstrating the efficacy of modified CDs in detecting IgG and IgM antibodies of COVID-19 patients, this research could pave the way for innovative diagnostic approaches, thus advancing the field of nanoparticle-based detection methods for infectious diseases. Considering all the advantages of this developed APBA-CDs marker, it emerges as a promising auxiliary diagnostic method for COVID-19. It can potentially be crucial in global efforts to contain the ongoing pandemic.

The synthesis of APBA-CDs used 2-Aminophenyl Boronate Acid (C6H8BNO2, 97 %), Citric Acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH, 1 M), Hydrochloric Acid (HCl, 1 M), and Ethanol (96 %) that were purchased from Merck KGaA, (Darmstadt, Germany). The assay marker was obtained using various samples, including blood plasma with positive IgG and IgM antibodies for COVID-19, blood plasma with negative IgG and IgM antibodies for COVID-19, healthy blood plasma taken before the pandemic patient collecting from Institute of Tropical Disease, Universitas Airlangga (Surabaya, Indonesia) and distilled water. All chemicals used were analytical grade.

APBA-CDs were prepared following previous studies [15]. Experimentally, a precise combination of APBA (184 mg) and citric acid (CA, 216 mg) was meticulously mixed and placed in a round bottom flask. Subsequently, the system was heated up to 270℃ for four hours to allow the mixture's pyrolysis carbonization process, resulting in the dark brown solid (APBA-CDs). Further, the colloidal solution of the CDs was achieved by dissolving the solid on sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH, 1 N). For the purification process, the solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 30 minutes to eliminate larger particles and was subsequently dialyzed using a cellulose dialysis bag by Orange Scientific CelluSep (MWCO, 1000 Da, Braine-l′Alleud, Belgium) for 24 h. Ultimately, the APBA-CD solution was collected for subsequent utilization.

Characterization of APBA-CDs compounds was carried with several instruments. UV-Vis spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu 1800 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an infrared (IR) tracer-100 (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffractograms were recorded using an D6 Phaser XRD Analyzer (Bruker, MA, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were performed on a PerkinElmer LS 55 spectrofluorometer (CT, USA)with a 20-kW xenon lamp. An atomic force microscope image was obtained using the scanning probe AFM5500M instrument (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Raman spectrum was analyzed on the MRS-320 Raman Instrument system (Horiba Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). The APBA-CDs marker assay for COVID-19 virus antibodies was carried out by measuring blood plasma's absorbance and fluorescence level, which indicated a positive result for COVID-19 antibodies with the APBA-CDs marker.

The optimization of APBA-CDs was assessed under varying pH levels and salt concentrations. The initial step in testing colloidal stability at a specific pH variance is to dissolve APBA-CDs in NaOH (1 M) until complete dissolution. The solution was adjusted to a pH range of 3–12 by adding HCl (1 M) and NaOH (1 M). Observations were recorded at 0, 12, and 24 hours. The turbidity of the APBA-CDs was measured using a Velp Scientifica TB1 turbidimeter (Velate MB, Italy) to detect the formation of aggregates on APBA-CDs at different pH levels, which indicates their instability under certain conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Marker Assay on COVID-19 Virus Antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 40 blood plasma samples were tested for the APBA-CDs compound as a marker candidate to detect COVID-19-specific antibodies (IgG/IgM). Three categories were identified using a commercial rapid test kit: 1. IgG (+) and IgM (-) (K1); 2. IgG (-) and IgM (+) (K2); and 3. IgG (+) and IgM (+) (K3). This test uses blood plasma from healthy volunteers collected before and during the COVID-19 pandemic as a negative control for IgG (-) and IgM (-) (K4), and the COVID-19 pandemic (K5). All the blood plasma samples were from the Institute of Tropical Diseases, Airlangga University. About 5 µL of blood plasma was added to the test well plate, followed by APBA-CDs (45 µL) addition with a pH of 7 in each well. The mixture is homogenized and sealed using a cover. The sample is then incubated at 37°C for 1–2 hours before measuring absorbance and fluorescence levels using Promega GloMax® Discover Microplate Reader (WI, USA).

We have proposed a pyrolysis method of an organic carbon source in a furnace to synthesize APBA-CDs. Pyrolysis is a rapid and straightforward method of synthesizing CDs at high temperatures and pressure. In addition, pyrolysis is an efficient and low-cost method that can be carried out at any time and produces a high quantum yield [23]. To prepare APBA-CDs, APBA and CA were synthesized using the proposed method, forming a black, hard powder under UV light (as displayed in Figure S1). The precursors encounter dehydration and carbonization under high temperature and pressure conditions ( Fig. 1). This phenomenon accelerates the loading process of heteroatoms (B and N), resulting in APBA-CDs that possess remarkable optoelectronic properties compared to the initial material [24].

In continuation of our studies for preparing fluorescence APBA-CDs and considering their potential as a marker, herein, we synthesized APBA-CDs according to our previous reports [25]. In this phase, a comprehensive investigation of the optical properties of APBA-CDs was conducted, involving fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra analyses. Dissolving the synthesized APBA-CDs solids in NaOH revealed a clear and colored solution of APBA-CDs, a crucial step for analysis with a UV–vis spectrophotometer within the 200–800 nm range as shown in Fig. 2 a. The spectral results revealed a shoulder peak for APBA-CDs, indicating the excitation of CDs at a wavelength of 315–360 nm and signifying an n-π* transition. The peak at wavelength 262 nm also suggested a π-π* CC transition. In contrast, a broad peak around wavelength 360 nm indicated an n-π* transition involving CO and CN functionalities on the surface of APBA-CDs [25]. Moreover, the APBA-CDs demonstrated significant absorption peaks at 360 nm, indicating that this specific wavelength could be utilized as the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 a, red line). APBA-CDs generated emission spectra with a 450-nm peak by employing an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (see inset Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, the absorption and emission spectrum generated by the APBA-CDs is prominently elevated, as illustrated in Figures S2a and S2b. The emission from APBA-CDs is due to the presence of an electron-hole pair of nitrogen and boron atoms [15]. The APBA-CDs solution's Photoluminescence Quantum Yield (PLQY) was determined to be 24.3 %, significantly higher than the 0.28 % of the CA-CDs, by calculating it with a quinine sulfate reference solution (PLQY = 54.6 %). The PLQY value is calculated using the following formula:
(1)

S
Q
R
=
E
S
E
R
×
A

A

×
n

2
n

2
where Q
S and Q
R represent the quantum yield of sample and reference (Ф), E
S and E
R signify the emission intensity of the sample and reference, A
R and A
S demonstrate reference and sample absorbance, n
S and n
R represent indices refractive of sample and reference, respectively. ER Upon analysis at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 to 500 nm, the as-synthesized APBA-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence characteristics, as illustrated in Fig. 2 b, which are also similar to the control CA-CDs (Figure S3). The broad emission peak of carbon nanodots arises from their diverse energy levels. Notably, the APBA-CDs exhibit intense fluorescence at 360 nm excitation. This feature is inherent to carbon nanodots, reflecting their size and surface characteristics [26].

The molecular structural characteristics of the synthesized APBA-CDs were analyzed through several necessary characterizations. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses were conducted to assess the CDs' surface morphology and three-dimensional structure quantitatively. Fig. 3 a depicts a monodisperse particle morphology. Using the ImageJ software analysis, the average diameter of APBA was calculated to be 7.3 ± 0.06 nm (Fig. 3 b), supported by the 2D and 3D topographical images of APBA-CDs (as depicted in Fig. 3 c and Figure S4). The Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SEAD) pattern exhibits a crystalline nature for the synthesized CDs, confirming the presence of numerous graphene layers (Fig. 3 d). The crystalline phase of boronic acid-containing CDs underwent comprehensive analysis through X-ray diffraction (XRD) observation, as depicted in Fig. 3 e. XRD serves as an instrumental technique for analyzing the crystal structure of APBA-CDs nanoparticles. The analysis involved the utilization of a Cu Kα beam (λ=1.5406 Å) within a 2θ range spanning 5˚ to 60˚. The XRD diffractogram of APBA-CDs shows distinctive lines, confirming the crystalline phase of boronic acid-containing carbon dots. After acquiring the post-diffractogram, a comparative analysis was done using the JCPDS database. The obtained database aligned symmetrically with the XRD diffractogram of the APBA-CDs compound. Specific crystal identifications, such as fullerene, nitrogen, carbon, and amino crystals, were derived from the JCPDS values associated with each crystal (JCPDS 47–0787, JCPDS 31–1986, JCPDS 23–1804, and JCPDS 12–0903, respectively) [27]. As a result of the presence of graphene-like structure, crystalline carbon, nitrogen, and amino crystalline structures within the APBA-CDs compound, multiple peaks were observed. The XRD diffractogram of APBA-CDs was observed at an angle of 18.73˚. Further investigation into the graphene-like structure within APBA-CDs was carried out through Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3 f). Raman spectroscopy is crucial for analyzing CDs' D and G bands. It reveals two prominent bands: the D band, representing a carbon atom binding to a group, and the G band, representing a sp2 hybridized carbon atom vibration [28]. To determine the purity of the CDs, we analyze the ratio between the D and G bands. The identification process for Raman spectroscopy begins by detecting the D band at 1350 cm−1, indicating sp3 hybridization in the carbon matrix. Moreover, the presence of the G band, observed at a wavelength of 1578 cm−1, suggests the existence of sp2 hybridization in carbon within the APBA-CD compound [29]. The ID/IG ratio for APBA-CDs was calculated to be 0.95, indicating the coexistence of sp2 and sp3 hybridization. The Raman spectroscopy analysis provides valuable insights into the structural composition of APBA-CDs, particularly regarding the nature of carbon hybridization within the carbon nanodots. In order to investigate the chemical binding in APBA-CDs, a detailed Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis was initiated to compare the spectra of CA, APBA, and APBA-CDs. The analysis covered the 400–4000 cm−1 range (as depicted in Fig. 3 g). The FTIR spectrum of CA reveals distinctive peaks at 3292, 1711, and 1391 cm−1, associated with O-H stretching, CO, and C-O stretching, respectively [30]. Also, APBA's FTIR spectra exhibit peaks at 2868, 1711, and 1391 cm−1, corresponding to -N-H stretching, B-O, and C-B, respectively. It is evident from the absence of CO peak in the APBA spectrum that it is from CA. Additionally, the nitrogen vibration indicated by a 3215 cm−1 band signifies the presence of hydroxyl groups in the APBA-CDs compound [24]. The compound APBA-CDs exhibits prominent bands at wavelengths 2359, 1715, 1379, 1194, and 1070 cm−1, providing evidence of the NH, CO, BO, B-OH, and CB stretch groups, respectively.

Elemental composition analysis and chemical state examination of APBA-CDs were performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as depicted in Figure S5. The successful synthesis of APBA-CDs is confirmed by characteristic peaks for B, C, N, and O at binding energies of 190, 284, 399, and 531 eV, respectively. According to the XPS spectra, the atomic ratio of the elements is as follows: 7.79 % B, 43.53 % C, 1.20 % N, and 48.48 % O (Table S1). The successful integration of the B1s region was deconvoluted, signifying C-B and B-O bonding ( Fig. 4 a). As delineated in Fig. 4 b, the C1s spectra of APBA-CDs comprised numerous peaks representing C-C, CC/CN, and CO. Subsequently, from the N1s spectra of APBA-CDs, two integrated signals represent graphitic and pyridinic N (Fig. 4 c). As illustrated in Fig. 4 d, the spectra of O1s are divided into two discernible peaks, each associated with C–O, C–O, or CO. Thus, results support the effective incorporation of dopant ions into the CDs framework.

In the subsequent investigation, the colloidal stability of the APBA-CDs prepared was examined, considering factors such as pH, ionic strength, and temperature. The formation of aggregates indicated that the APBA-CDs exhibited instability under specific pH conditions. To assess stability, the existing APBA-CDs experienced testing at various pH levels, ranging from 3 to 14, over 24 hours, as depicted in Figure S6 (Supporting Information). The test results showed no formation of precipitates or aggregates at pH 6–10 over the 24-hour duration. Turbidity measurements at pH 6–10 were between 2.25 and 2.80 NTU, indicating stability within this pH range. Instability of APBA-CDs was observed at pH 3–5 and 11–14, with resultant precipitate formation. However, these compounds were stable within the pH range of 6–10, which provides a valuable reference for future research. The turbidity measurement of APBA-CDs on varied NaCl concentrations up to 24 days of observation is depicted in Figure S7. According to the graph, no sediments or aggregates were formed during the observations. Consequently, we may conclude that the APBA-CDs solution remains stable on the ionic strength test (NaCl concentration from 0 to 0.8 M). The stability observation on varied pH and NaCl prove that the APBA-CDs save on human biological condition, where physiological ionic strength and pH are around 0.15 M and pH 4.5 to pH 8, respectively [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of APBCA-CDs Efficacy in Targeting COVID-19 Virus Antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: During the marker test, the absorbance and fluorescence results of the APBA-CDs compound were compared with those of plasma alone without using any compound markers (as illustrated in Fig. 5). The absorbance test was conducted using four different wavelengths such as 360 nm (Fig. 5 a), 380 nm (Fig. 5 b), 400 nm (Fig. 5 c), and 450 nm (Fig. 5 d). The results showed a significant increase in absorbance levels for categories K1 (IgG positive and IgM negative), K2 (IgG negative and IgM positive), and K3 (IgG positive and IgM positive). The notable enhancement is attributed to the introduction of CDs into the plasma, as CDs exhibit higher absorbance levels than plasma. These results suggest that APBA-CDs are effectively bound to antibodies in blood plasma, indicating a positive presence of COVID-19. Furthermore, boronic acid, employed as a guide for CDs to the COVID-19 virus, selectively reacts with 1,2- or 1,3-cis diol groups, resembling the existing gp120 binding agent in the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) [15]. Moreover, a previous study confirmed that diol groups could block viral processes within the host. It was confirmed that both IgG and IgM consist of a large number of hydroxyl group and cis-diol [31], formation of the boronated complex resulted from the boronic acid of APBA and cis-diols of the antibodies became the fundamental reason for the effectiveness of the APBA-CDs marker and is potentially developed as a specific marker for detecting the COVID-19 virus.

Upon transitioning to the fluorescence test, a consistent increase in fluorescence levels was observed across all categories, indicating a substantial rise in categories K1, K2, and K3 due to the addition of CDs to plasma ( Fig. 6). APBA-CDs show higher fluorescence levels than plasma and have been found to bind effectively with the COVID-19 antibodies present in positive plasma samples [32]. Although an increase was observed in categories K4 and K5, it was less prominent, suggesting that APBA-CDs did not bind to specific antibodies. The fluorescence level of K5 was higher than that of K4, aligning with Erdem et al.'s study, indicating the possible presence of COVID-19 antibodies in the plasma undetected by existing rapid tests [2]. The study highlights the challenge of detecting natural antibodies in COVID-19 patients who have recovered, emphasizing the limitations of rapid test kits in capturing specific antibodies. These finding shows the excellent potential of the APBA-CDs on COVID-19 detection, which is comparable with previous report (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical Evaluation of APBA-CDs using ANOVA and PCA
ce:section.ce:para: Statistical tests, including analysis of variance (ANOVA) and principal component analysis (PCA) tests, were conducted to assess the validity of the data [33–35]. Fig. 7 a illustrates the ANOVA test to indicate the validity of both absorbance and fluorescence data, as the calculated F value surpasses the critical F value, leading to the rejection of H0. Additionally, the PCA test depicted in Fig. 7 b underscores the robustness of data in categories K1, K2, K3, K4, and K5. Notably, all data exhibit high variance values of 0.997, 0.984, 0.875, 0.803, and 0.738, respectively. The PCA test further reveals the specificity of APBA-CDs to IgG+ or K1. This specificity is supported by the sensitivity and specificity calculations for APBA-CDs against IgG Covid-19 antibodies, producing 74 % and 53 %, respectively. Compared to IgG antibodies, assessing against IgM results in lower sensitivity and specificity rates of 63 % and 27 %, respectively. These results supporting previous data on Fig. 5 and Fig. 6. indicate that the performance of APBA-CDs on marking IgG antibody was higher than those on IgM. Moreover, the accumulation of the sample on ring of PCA proves that the APBA-CDs well-response on both IgG and IgM.

In summary, APBA-CDs were successfully synthesized via pyrolysis, as confirmed by various analyses, including AFM, XRD, UV-Vis, FTIR, Raman, and PL. The successful synthesis of APBA-CDs affirms promising potential as marker candidates for detecting COVID-19 antibodies, particularly IgG antibodies. The APBA-CDs marker demonstrated high sensitivity, indicated by increased absorbance and fluorescence levels in blood plasma, signifying positive COVID-19 antibodies. The findings underscore the viability of APBA-CDs as a robust tool for the sensitive and specific detection of COVID-19 antibodies. This research provides valuable insights for developing practical COVID-19 diagnostic tools noninvasively and further clinical exploration of APBA-CDs for antibody detection.

Ni Luh Ayu Megasari: Data curation. Brian Eka Rachman: Project administration. Nasronudin Nasronudin: Funding acquisition. Jia-yaw Chang: Visualization. Mochamad Zakki Fahmi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Siti Febtria Asrini Sugito: Data curation. Siti Qamariyah Khairunisa: Formal analysis. Aswandi Wibrianto: Writing – original draft. Yudha Juli Saputra: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Ministry of Research, Technology, and Higher Education Republic of Indonesia under contract No. 928/UN3.15/PT/2022 and 010/E5/PG.02.00.PT/2022.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2024.116242.
